text: Overlapping pressures from anthropogenic climate change and global health security mean healthcare needs to act fast to address its footprint. Medical imaging, one of the carbon intenstive branches of medicine, is characterised by large amounts of energy used, single-use plastic products and an unprecedented amount of digital data produced. We review the major contributors in radiology such as the idling mode of MRI and CT machines and address "Scope 3" emissions related to the global supply chains that support radiology. Practical and pragmatic steps towards a greener radiology can be taken by all stakeholders in the form of smart patient positioning, "drinking moderate amounts of digital alcohol" and resource efficiency through the implementation of the principles of the circular economy into healthcare practices such as optimal use of existing medical imaging infrastructure and resource-efficient waste handling. The authors call upon radiologists and healthcare personnel to rise to the challenge and lead the way for sustainable change in the workplace.
